title: LITENDIAN warning is backwards.
body:
Thanks for taking the time to report this.

### Can you attach an example that shows the issue?  (Must be openly licensed, ideally in test_regress format.)
![Verilator](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15210279/220150652-1d3bb681-abf8-4c8c-8378-e71f18136096.jpg)


### What 'verilator --version' are you using?  Did you try it with the git master version?
Verilator 5.006 2023-01-22 rev v5.006-31-g8cc31c6cf

### What OS and distribution are you using?
WSL2 Ubuntu 18.04


### May we assist you in trying to fix this in Verilator yourself?
Sure.


# Problem
The current way LITENDIAN is named and described is backwards from what is actually being defined in the Verilog vector. 

# Explanation
In IEEE 1364-2005, under section 4.3.1 Specifying vectors:
> The range specification gives addresses to the individual bits in a multibit net or reg. The most significant
> bit specified by the msb constant expression is the left-hand value in the range, and the least significant bit
> specified by the lsb constant expression is the right-hand value in the range.

In IEEE 1800-2017, under section 6.9.1 Specifying vectors:
> The range specification ([msb_constant_expression : lsb_constant_expression]) gives addresses to the
> individual bits in a multibit reg, logic, or bit vector. The most significant bit, specified by the msb
> constant expression, is the left-hand value in the range, and the least significant bit, specified by the lsb
> constant expression, is the right-hand value in the range.

If I declare the following vector:

`reg [0:7] c;`

Then the most-significant-bit (msb) is at index 0, making this register bit-wise big endian.
In other words, accessing index 0 (the first bit) of this vector yields the msb.
By definition, seeing the msb first means that this is big bit endian.

The current description of the warning says the following:

[LITENDIAN](https://verilator.org/guide/latest/warnings.html#cmdoption-arg-LITENDIAN)
![verilator3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15210279/220161219-762b88dc-a259-42fe-a192-33a4ae69b0fc.png)


I agree that declaring a vector like this 
`reg [7:0] c;`
has become the common practice, but this is little endian bit numbering.


# Example
I have created a simple module to support my explanation:
```
module top;
  reg [3:0] little;
  reg [0:3] big;

  initial begin
    little = 4'b10xz;
    big   = 4'b10xz;
  end

  integer i;
  
  always @(*) begin
    for (i = 0; i < 8; i = i + 1) begin
        $display("little %b\tbig %b", little[i], big[i]);
      end
    end

endmodule
```

Here is the output of the simulation:

> little z        big 1
> little x        big 0
> little 0        big x
> little 1        big z
> little x        big x
> little x        big x
> little x        big x
> little x        big x

Here is Verilator linting incorrectly warning me about my code:
![Verilator2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15210279/220159079-8b3f77b4-be4f-4cb2-a652-9035f5ad5ebb.jpg)

In the example, we see 
- the **lsb** is z
- the **msb** is 1
- the **little** vector printing the **least-significant-bit (lsb)** first. This is little bit endian.
- the **big** vector printing the **most-significant-bit (msb)** first. This is big bit endian.
- Verilator linting giving a warning that marks big as "little bit endian"

# Conclusion
I disagree that you should warn users about reversing the index numbering in vectors. There can be performance improvements for comparing bits in two bytes by processing the most-significant-bits first. A design could immediately stop processing bits as soon as it encounters the first most-significant-bits that don't match between two bytes and provide an answer on which value is larger.

This warning should either be removed or re-written to explain that this is **Big endian bit numbering** and that **little endian bit numbering is now the overwhelming standard.**

It should also say which standard and **where** it says in the IEEE standard that 
`"... the bits will likely be backward from what people may expect"`
or remove that kind of misleading language from the documentation.
